    21→### Epic: Analysis-Ready Export (ARE) System
    22→**Story Points: 8**
    23→
    24→#### ARE-001: Analysis-Ready Export (Tidy CSV)
    25→**Points: 4 | Priority: Critical**
    26→
    27→**Description:** Implement comprehensive data export system that produces analysis-ready datasets in multiple formats.
    28→
    29→**Technical Requirements:**
    30→- BIDS-compatible CSV export with standardized column naming
    31→- Parquet format support for large datasets and better performance
    32→- JSON export for programmatic data access
    33→- Data validation and integrity checking
    34→- Metadata preservation and documentation
    35→
    36→**Acceptance Criteria:**
    37→- [ ] CSV export follows tidy data principles with standardized columns
    38→- [ ] Parquet export maintains data types and compression for large datasets
    39→- [ ] JSON export includes complete metadata and experiment configuration
    40→- [ ] Data validation prevents corrupt or incomplete exports
    41→- [ ] Export format documentation enables easy data loading
    42→- [ ] BIDS validator passes all exported datasets
    43→
    44→**Data Export Implementation:**
    45→```typescript
    46→// @psyforge/export
    47→export class AnalysisReadyExporter {
    48→  private experiment: CompiledExperiment;
    49→  private rawData: ExperimentData[];
    50→  private metadata: ExperimentMetadata;
    51→  
    52→  constructor(experiment: CompiledExperiment, data: ExperimentData[], metadata: ExperimentMetadata) {
    53→    this.experiment = experiment;
    54→    this.rawData = data;
    55→    this.metadata = metadata;
    56→  }
    57→  
    58→  // Export to tidy CSV format
    59→  async exportCSV(options: CSVExportOptions = {}): Promise<CSVExportResult> {
    60→    const tidyData = this.transformToTidyFormat(this.rawData);
    61→    const validated = this.validateData(tidyData);
    62→    
    63→    const csv = this.generateCSV(validated, {
    64→      includeHeaders: options.includeHeaders ?? true,
    65→      delimiter: options.delimiter ?? ',',
    66→      encoding: options.encoding ?? 'utf-8'
    67→    });
    68→    
    69→    const metadata = this.generateDataDictionary(validated);
    70→    
    71→    return {
    72→      data: csv,
    73→      metadata: metadata,
    74→      fileName: this.generateFileName('csv'),
    75→      schema: this.generateJSONSchema(validated)
    76→    };
    77→  }
    78→  
    79→  // Export to Parquet for performance
    80→  async exportParquet(): Promise<ParquetExportResult> {
    81→    const tidyData = this.transformToTidyFormat(this.rawData);
    82→    const validated = this.validateData(tidyData);
    83→    
    84→    // Convert to Arrow Table for Parquet serialization
    85→    const table = this.convertToArrowTable(validated);
    86→    const parquetBuffer = await this.serializeToParquet(table);
    87→    
    88→    return {
    89→      data: parquetBuffer,
    90→      fileName: this.generateFileName('parquet'),
    91→      schema: table.schema,
    92→      compressionRatio: this.calculateCompressionRatio(validated, parquetBuffer)
    93→    };
    94→  }
    95→  
    96→  // Transform raw experiment data to tidy format
    97→  private transformToTidyFormat(rawData: ExperimentData[]): TidyDataRow[] {
    98→    return rawData.map((trial, index) => ({
    99→      // Participant information
   100→      participant_id: trial.participantId,
   101→      session_id: trial.sessionId,
   102→      age: trial.demographics?.age,
   103→      gender: trial.demographics?.gender,
   104→      
   105→      // Trial identifiers
   106→      trial_number: index + 1,
   107→      trial_id: trial.trialId,
   108→      trial_type: trial.trialType,
   109→      block_id: trial.blockId,
   110→      timeline_id: trial.timelineId,
   111→      
   112→      // Experimental conditions
   113→      condition: trial.condition,
   114→      condition_level_1: trial.conditionLevels?.[0],
   115→      condition_level_2: trial.conditionLevels?.[1], 
   116→      condition_level_3: trial.conditionLevels?.[2],
   117→      
   118→      // Stimulus information
   119→      stimulus_id: trial.stimulusId,
   120→      stimulus_type: trial.stimulusType,
   121→      stimulus_content: trial.stimulusContent,
   122→      stimulus_file: trial.stimulusFile,
   123→      stimulus_onset_time: trial.stimulusOnsetTime,
   124→      stimulus_offset_time: trial.stimulusOffsetTime,
   125→      stimulus_duration: trial.stimulusDuration,
   126→      
   127→      // Response data
   128→      response_key: trial.responseKey,
   129→      response_time: trial.responseTime,
   130→      response_accuracy: trial.responseAccuracy ? 1 : 0,
   131→      response_timeout: trial.responseTimeout ? 1 : 0,
   132→      reaction_time: trial.reactionTime,
   133→      
   134→      // Timing validation
   135→      stimulus_onset_jitter: trial.timingValidation?.onsetJitter,
   136→      frame_drops: trial.timingValidation?.frameDrops,
   137→      refresh_rate: trial.timingValidation?.refreshRate,
   138→      
   139→      // Device information
   140→      input_device: trial.inputDevice,
   141→      device_latency: trial.deviceLatency,
   142→      
   143→      // Experiment metadata
   144→      experiment_name: this.experiment.name,
   145→      experiment_version: this.experiment.version,
   146→      forgescript_version: this.metadata.forgescriptVersion,
   147→      
   148→      // Timestamps
   149→      trial_start_time: trial.trialStartTime,
   150→      trial_end_time: trial.trialEndTime,
   151→      experiment_start_time: this.metadata.experimentStartTime,
   152→      
   153→      // Custom variables (flattened)
   154→      ...this.flattenCustomVariables(trial.customVariables)
   155→    }));
   156→  }
   157→  
   158→  // Generate data dictionary for documentation
   159→  private generateDataDictionary(data: TidyDataRow[]): DataDictionary {
   160→    return {
   161→      participant_id: {
   162→        description: 'Unique identifier for participant',
   163→        type: 'string',
   164→        example: 'P001'
   165→      },
   166→      trial_number: {
   167→        description: 'Sequential trial number within experiment',
   168→        type: 'integer',
   169→        range: [1, data.length]
   170→      },
   171→      condition: {
   172→        description: 'Experimental condition or factor level',
   173→        type: 'categorical',
   174→        levels: [...new Set(data.map(d => d.condition))]
   175→      },
   176→      reaction_time: {
   177→        description: 'Response time in milliseconds from stimulus onset',
   178→        type: 'numeric',
   179→        unit: 'milliseconds',
   180→        missing_value_code: -999
   181→      },
   182→      response_accuracy: {
   183→        description: 'Response accuracy (1 = correct, 0 = incorrect)',
   184→        type: 'binary',
   185→        coding: { 0: 'incorrect', 1: 'correct' }
   186→      },
   187→      stimulus_onset_jitter: {
   188→        description: 'Measured timing precision for stimulus onset',
   189→        type: 'numeric',
   190→        unit: 'milliseconds',
   191→        note: 'Lower values indicate better timing precision'
   192→      }
   193→      // ... additional column descriptions
   194→    };
   195→  }
   196→}
   197→
   198→// BIDS-compatible directory structure
   199→export class BIDSExporter extends AnalysisReadyExporter {
   200→  async exportBIDS(): Promise<BIDSExportResult> {
   201→    const structure = await this.generateBIDSStructure();
   202→    
   203→    return {
   204→      structure: structure,
   205→      files: {
   206→        'participants.tsv': await this.generateParticipantsFile(),
   207→        'dataset_description.json': await this.generateDatasetDescription(),
   208→        'task-experiment_events.tsv': await this.exportCSV({ delimiter: '\t' }),
   209→        'task-experiment_bold.json': await this.generateTaskDescription(),
   210→        'README': await this.generateBIDSReadme(),
   211→        'CHANGES': await this.generateChangelog()
   212→      },
   213→      validator_passed: await this.validateBIDSCompliance(structure)
   214→    };
   215→  }
   216→  
   217→  private async generateBIDSStructure(): Promise<BIDSStructure> {
   218→    return {
   219→      root: '/',
   220→      subjects: this.getUniqueParticipants().map(participantId => ({
   221→        id: participantId,
   222→        sessions: this.getSessionsForParticipant(participantId),
   223→        behavioral_data: `sub-${participantId}/beh/`,
   224→        physiological_data: this.hasPhysiologicalData(participantId) ? 
   225→          `sub-${participantId}/physio/` : undefined
   226→      }))
   227→    };
   228→  }
   229→}
   230→```
   231→
   232→#### ARE-002: R Script Code Generation
   233→**Points: 4 | Priority: High**
   234→
   235→**Description:** Generate analysis-ready R and Python scripts that can immediately load and analyze exported data.
   236→
   237→**Technical Requirements:**
   238→- R script generation with appropriate statistical packages
   239→- Python script generation with pandas and scipy
   240→- Automatic detection of experimental design for correct analysis
   241→- Visualization code for common experimental plots
   242→- Statistical power analysis integration
   243→
   244→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   245→- [ ] Generated R scripts load data correctly with appropriate data types
   246→- [ ] Python scripts use pandas best practices for data analysis
   247→- [ ] Scripts include appropriate statistical tests for experimental design
   248→- [ ] Visualization code produces publication-quality plots
   249→- [ ] Power analysis code validates experimental design
   250→- [ ] Scripts include comprehensive comments and documentation
   251→
   252→**Script Generation Implementation:**
   253→```typescript
   254→// @psyforge/analysis-generator
   255→export class AnalysisScriptGenerator {
   256→  private experiment: CompiledExperiment;
   257→  private dataSchema: DataSchema;
   258→  private designType: ExperimentalDesign;
   259→  
   260→  constructor(experiment: CompiledExperiment, dataSchema: DataSchema) {
   261→    this.experiment = experiment;
   262→    this.dataSchema = dataSchema;
   263→    this.designType = this.detectExperimentalDesign(experiment);
   264→  }
   265→  
   266→  // Generate R analysis script
   267→  generateRScript(): string {
   268→    const script = new RScriptBuilder()
   269→      .addHeader(this.generateRHeader())
   270→      .addDataLoading(this.generateRDataLoading())
   271→      .addDataPreprocessing(this.generateRPreprocessing())
   272→      .addDescriptiveAnalysis(this.generateRDescriptives())
   273→      .addInferentialAnalysis(this.generateRInferentials())
   274→      .addVisualization(this.generateRPlots())
   275→      .addPowerAnalysis(this.generateRPowerAnalysis())
   276→      .build();
   277→    
   278→    return script;
   279→  }
   280→  
   281→  private generateRHeader(): string {
   282→    return `
   283→# ${this.experiment.name} - Analysis Script
   284→# Generated by Psycho-Forge on ${new Date().toISOString()}
   285→# 
   286→# This script performs standard analyses for the experimental design:
   287→# Design Type: ${this.designType.type}
   288→# Factors: ${this.designType.factors.join(', ')}
   289→# Dependent Variables: ${this.designType.dependentVariables.join(', ')}
   290→
   291→# Load required packages
   292→library(tidyverse)    # Data manipulation and visualization
   293→library(afex)         # ANOVA functions  
   294→library(emmeans)      # Post-hoc comparisons
   295→library(effectsize)   # Effect size calculations
   296→library(performance) # Model diagnostics
   297→library(report)       # Automated reporting
   298→library(pwr)          # Power analysis
   299→
   300→# Set options
   301→options(contrasts = c("contr.sum", "contr.poly"))  # Type III SS
   302→theme_set(theme_minimal())  # Clean ggplot theme
   303→`;
   304→  }
   305→  
   306→  private generateRDataLoading(): string {
   307→    return `
   308→# Load and prepare data
   309→data_file <- "${this.generateFileName('csv')}"
   310→raw_data <- read_csv(data_file, col_types = cols(
   311→  participant_id = col_character(),
   312→  trial_number = col_integer(),
   313→  condition = col_factor(),
   314→  response_accuracy = col_logical(),
   315→  reaction_time = col_double(),
   316→  .default = col_guess()
   317→))
   318→
   319→# Data validation
   320→cat("Data loaded successfully\\n")
   321→cat("Participants:", n_distinct(raw_data$participant_id), "\\n")
   322→cat("Total trials:", nrow(raw_data), "\\n")
   323→cat("Conditions:", paste(levels(raw_data$condition), collapse = ", "), "\\n")
   324→`;
   325→  }
   326→  
   327→  private generateRPreprocessing(): string {
   328→    return `
   329→# Data preprocessing
   330→clean_data <- raw_data %>%
   331→  # Remove practice trials if present
   332→  filter(!str_detect(timeline_id, "practice")) %>%
   333→  
   334→  # Remove trials with missing responses
   335→  filter(!is.na(reaction_time), !is.na(response_accuracy)) %>%
   336→  
   337→  # Remove unrealistic RTs (< 100ms or > 3000ms)
   338→  filter(reaction_time >= 100, reaction_time <= 3000) %>%
   339→  
   340→  # Remove trials with timing errors > 5ms
   341→  filter(abs(stimulus_onset_jitter) <= 5) %>%
   342→  
   343→  # Calculate participant-level exclusions
   344→  group_by(participant_id) %>%
   345→  mutate(
   346→    participant_accuracy = mean(response_accuracy, na.rm = TRUE),
   347→    participant_mean_rt = mean(reaction_time[response_accuracy], na.rm = TRUE)
   348→  ) %>%
   349→  ungroup() %>%
   350→  
   351→  # Exclude participants with < 60% accuracy
   352→  filter(participant_accuracy >= 0.60) %>%
   353→  
   354→  # Remove outlier RTs (2.5 SD from participant mean)
   355→  group_by(participant_id, condition) %>%
   356→  mutate(
   357→    rt_z = abs(scale(reaction_time)[,1])
   358→  ) %>%
   359→  filter(rt_z <= 2.5) %>%
   360→  ungroup()
   361→
   362→cat("After preprocessing:\\n")
   363→cat("Participants remaining:", n_distinct(clean_data$participant_id), "\\n")
   364→cat("Trials remaining:", nrow(clean_data), "\\n")
   365→cat("Data exclusion rate:", 
   366→    round((1 - nrow(clean_data) / nrow(raw_data)) * 100, 1), "%\\n")
   367→`;
   368→  }
   369→  
   370→  private generateRInferentials(): string {
   371→    if (this.designType.type === 'within_subjects') {
   372→      return this.generateWithinSubjectsANOVA();
   373→    } else if (this.designType.type === 'between_subjects') {
   374→      return this.generateBetweenSubjectsANOVA();
   375→    } else {
   376→      return this.generateMixedDesignANOVA();
   377→    }
   378→  }
   379→  
   380→  private generateWithinSubjectsANOVA(): string {
   381→    return `
   382→# Within-subjects ANOVA
   383→rt_model <- aov_ez(
   384→  id = "participant_id",
   385→  dv = "reaction_time", 
   386→  data = filter(clean_data, response_accuracy == TRUE),
   387→  within = c(${this.designType.factors.map(f => `"${f}"`).join(', ')})
   388→)
   389→
   390→# Model summary
   391→print(rt_model)
   392→print(report(rt_model))
   393→
   394→# Effect sizes
   395→print(eta_squared(rt_model, partial = TRUE))
   396→
   397→# Post-hoc comparisons
   398→rt_emmeans <- emmeans(rt_model, ~ ${this.designType.factors[0]})
   399→print(contrast(rt_emmeans, "pairwise", adjust = "bonferroni"))
   400→
   401→# Accuracy analysis
   402→acc_data <- clean_data %>%
   403→  group_by(participant_id, ${this.designType.factors.join(', ')}) %>%
   404→  summarise(accuracy = mean(response_accuracy), .groups = "drop")
   405→
   406→acc_model <- aov_ez(
   407→  id = "participant_id",
   408→  dv = "accuracy",
   409→  data = acc_data,
   410→  within = c(${this.designType.factors.map(f => `"${f}"`).join(', ')})
   411→)
   412→
   413→print(acc_model)
   414→print(report(acc_model))
   415→`;
   416→  }
   417→}
   418→
   419→// Python script generation
   420→export class PythonAnalysisGenerator {
   421→  generatePythonScript(): string {
   422→    return `
   423→# ${this.experiment.name} - Python Analysis Script
   424→# Generated by Psycho-Forge on ${new Date().toISOString()}
   425→
   426→import pandas as pd
   427→import numpy as np
   428→import scipy.stats as stats
   429→import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
   430→import seaborn as sns
   431→from scipy import stats
   432→import pingouin as pg
   433→
   434→# Load data
   435→df = pd.read_csv('${this.generateFileName('csv')}')
   436→
   437→# Data preprocessing
   438→df_clean = (df
   439→    .query('timeline_id != "practice"')  # Remove practice
   440→    .dropna(subset=['reaction_time', 'response_accuracy'])  # Remove missing
   441→    .query('100 <= reaction_time <= 3000')  # Remove outlier RTs
   442→    .query('abs(stimulus_onset_jitter) <= 5')  # Remove timing errors
   443→)
   444→
   445→# Participant-level filtering
   446→participant_accuracy = df_clean.groupby('participant_id')['response_accuracy'].mean()
   447→valid_participants = participant_accuracy[participant_accuracy >= 0.60].index
   448→df_clean = df_clean[df_clean['participant_id'].isin(valid_participants)]
   449→
   450→print(f"Data loaded: {len(df)} trials, {df['participant_id'].nunique()} participants")
   451→print(f"After cleaning: {len(df_clean)} trials, {df_clean['participant_id'].nunique()} participants")
   452→
   453→# Descriptive statistics
   454→print("\\nDescriptive Statistics:")
   455→desc_stats = df_clean.groupby('condition').agg({
   456→    'reaction_time': ['mean', 'std', 'count'],
   457→    'response_accuracy': 'mean'
   458→}).round(3)
   459→print(desc_stats)
   460→
   461→# Inferential statistics
   462→if len(df_clean['condition'].unique()) == 2:
   463→    # Paired t-test for two conditions
   464→    rt_correct = df_clean[df_clean['response_accuracy'] == True]
   465→    
   466→    condition_1 = rt_correct[rt_correct['condition'] == df_clean['condition'].unique()[0]]['reaction_time']
   467→    condition_2 = rt_correct[rt_correct['condition'] == df_clean['condition'].unique()[1]]['reaction_time']
   468→    
   469→    t_stat, p_val = stats.ttest_rel(condition_1, condition_2)
   470→    effect_size = (condition_1.mean() - condition_2.mean()) / np.sqrt((condition_1.var() + condition_2.var()) / 2)
   471→    
   472→    print(f"\\nPaired t-test: t = {t_stat:.3f}, p = {p_val:.3f}")
   473→    print(f"Cohen's d = {effect_size:.3f}")
   474→    
   475→else:
   476→    # Repeated measures ANOVA for multiple conditions
   477→    rm_anova = pg.rm_anova(data=df_clean[df_clean['response_accuracy'] == True], 
   478→                          dv='reaction_time', within='condition', subject='participant_id')
   479→    print("\\nRepeated Measures ANOVA:")
   480→    print(rm_anova)
   481→
   482→# Visualization
   483→plt.figure(figsize=(12, 5))
   484→
   485→plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
   486→sns.boxplot(data=df_clean[df_clean['response_accuracy'] == True], 
   487→           x='condition', y='reaction_time')
   488→plt.title('Reaction Time by Condition')
   489→plt.ylabel('Reaction Time (ms)')
   490→
   491→plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
   492→acc_by_condition = df_clean.groupby(['participant_id', 'condition'])['response_accuracy'].mean().reset_index()
   493→sns.barplot(data=acc_by_condition, x='condition', y='response_accuracy', ci=95)
   494→plt.title('Accuracy by Condition')
   495→plt.ylabel('Accuracy')
   496→plt.ylim(0, 1)
   497→
   498→plt.tight_layout()
   499→plt.savefig('analysis_results.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
   500→plt.show()
   501→`;
   502→  }
   503→}
   504→```
   505→
   506→---
   507→
   508→### Epic: Methods Section Generator (MSG)
   509→**Story Points: 6**
   510→
   511→#### MSG-001: Methods Section Generator
   512→**Points: 4 | Priority: High**
   513→
   514→**Description:** Automatically generate publication-ready methods sections from experiment specifications.
   515→
   516→**Technical Requirements:**
   517→- Natural language generation from experiment AST
   518→- APA style formatting and citations
   519→- Technical specification documentation
   520→- Customizable templates for different publication venues
   521→- Integration with common reference management systems
   522→
   523→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   524→- [ ] Generated methods sections follow APA formatting guidelines
   525→- [ ] Technical specifications accurately described from experiment code
   526→- [ ] Participant information and procedures clearly documented
   527→- [ ] Analysis plan appropriately described
   528→- [ ] Output formats support Word, LaTeX, and Markdown
   529→- [ ] Citations automatically formatted and linked
   530→
   531→**Methods Generation Implementation:**
   532→```typescript
   533→// @psyforge/methods-generator
   534→export class MethodsGenerator {
   535→  private experiment: CompiledExperiment;
   536→  private template: MethodsTemplate;
   537→  private citations: CitationManager;
   538→  
   539→  constructor(experiment: CompiledExperiment, template: MethodsTemplate = 'apa') {
   540→    this.experiment = experiment;
   541→    this.template = MethodsTemplates[template];
   542→    this.citations = new CitationManager();
   543→  }
   544→  
   545→  generateMethodsSection(): MethodsSection {
   546→    return {
   547→      participants: this.generateParticipantsSection(),
   548→      apparatus: this.generateApparatusSection(), 
   549→      stimuli: this.generateStimuliSection(),
   550→      procedure: this.generateProcedureSection(),
   551→      design: this.generateDesignSection(),
   552→      dataAnalysis: this.generateDataAnalysisSection(),
   553→      citations: this.citations.getReferences()
   554→    };
   555→  }
   556→  
   557→  private generateParticipantsSection(): string {
   558→    const recruitment = this.experiment.recruitment;
   559→    
   560→    return this.template.format('participants', {
   561→      sampleSize: recruitment?.target?.n || '[N to be determined]',
   562→      ageRange: recruitment?.criteria?.age ? 
   563→        `${recruitment.criteria.age.min}-${recruitment.criteria.age.max} years` : 
   564→        '[age range to be specified]',
   565→      exclusionCriteria: this.formatExclusionCriteria(recruitment?.criteria),
   566→      compensation: recruitment?.compensation ? 
   567→        `$${recruitment.compensation.base} ${recruitment.compensation.currency}` : 
   568→        '[compensation to be specified]',
   569→      recruitmentMethod: this.formatRecruitmentMethod(recruitment?.providers),
   570→      ethicsApproval: '[IRB approval number to be added]'
   571→    });
   572→  }
   573→  
   574→  private generateApparatusSection(): string {
   575→    const devices = this.experiment.devices || [];
   576→    const timing = this.experiment.timingRequirements;
   577→    
   578→    return this.template.format('apparatus', {
   579→      computer: 'Standard laboratory computer with [specifications to be added]',
   580→      monitor: `${timing?.targetRefreshRate || 60}Hz LCD monitor`,
   581→      software: `Psycho-Forge ${this.experiment.forgescriptVersion} ${this.citations.cite('psycho-forge')}`,
   582→      inputDevices: devices.length > 0 ? 
   583→        devices.map(d => d.description).join(', ') : 
   584→        'Standard computer keyboard',
   585→      timingPrecision: timing?.guaranteedPrecision ? 
   586→        `Stimulus timing precision verified to ${timing.guaranteedPrecision}ms` : 
   587→        'High-precision stimulus timing validated',
   588→      additionalEquipment: this.formatAdditionalEquipment(devices)
   589→    });
   590→  }
   591→  
   592→  private generateStimuliSection(): string {
   593→    const stimuli = this.extractStimuliFromExperiment();
   594→    
   595→    const stimuliDescription = stimuli.map(stimulus => {
   596→      switch (stimulus.type) {
   597→        case 'text':
   598→          return `Text stimuli were presented in ${stimulus.fontSize}pt ${stimulus.fontFamily} font, colored ${stimulus.color}`;
   599→        case 'image':
   600→          return `Image stimuli (${stimulus.format}) were ${stimulus.dimensions} pixels`;
   601→        case 'audio':
   602→          return `Audio stimuli were ${stimulus.sampleRate}Hz ${stimulus.format} files`;
   603→        default:
   604→          return `${stimulus.type} stimuli were presented`;
   605→      }
   606→    }).join('. ');
   607→    
   608→    return this.template.format('stimuli', {
   609→      description: stimuliDescription,
   610→      timing: this.describeStimulusTiming(),
   611→      positioning: this.describeStimulusPositioning(),
   612→      counterbalancing: this.describeCounterbalancing()
   613→    });
   614→  }
   615→  
   616→  private generateProcedureSection(): string {
   617→    const timelines = this.experiment.timelines;
   618→    
   619→    const procedureSteps = timelines.map(timeline => {
   620→      const trialCount = timeline.trials?.length || 0;
   621→      const description = timeline.description || `${timeline.id} phase`;
   622→      
   623→      return `${description} consisted of ${trialCount} trials`;
   624→    }).join('. ');
   625→    
   626→    return this.template.format('procedure', {
   627→      overview: procedureSteps,
   628→      trialStructure: this.describeTrialStructure(),
   629→      timing: this.describeOverallTiming(),
   630→      instructions: this.formatInstructions(),
   631→      breaks: this.describeBreaks()
   632→    });
   633→  }
   634→  
   635→  private generateDataAnalysisSection(): string {
   636→    const design = this.experiment.designType;
   637→    const dvs = this.experiment.dependentVariables;
   638→    
   639→    return this.template.format('dataAnalysis', {
   640→      exclusionCriteria: this.formatDataExclusionCriteria(),
   641→      dependentVariables: dvs.join(', '),
   642→      statisticalTest: this.recommendStatisticalTest(design),
   643→      alphaLevel: '0.05',
   644→      corrections: 'Bonferroni correction for multiple comparisons',
   645→      software: `R version ${this.citations.cite('r-core')} with packages afex ${this.citations.cite('afex')} and emmeans ${this.citations.cite('emmeans')}`
   646→    });
   647→  }
   648→}
   649→
   650→// Template system for different publication formats
   651→export const MethodsTemplates = {
   652→  apa: {
   653→    format(section: string, data: any): string {
   654→      switch (section) {
   655→        case 'participants':
   656→          return `
   657→**Participants**
   658→
   659→${data.sampleSize} participants (age range: ${data.ageRange}) were recruited via ${data.recruitmentMethod}. Participants were compensated ${data.compensation} for their time. Exclusion criteria included ${data.exclusionCriteria}. The study was approved by the institutional review board (${data.ethicsApproval}).
   660→`;
   661→        
   662→        case 'apparatus':
   663→          return `
   664→**Apparatus**
   665→
   666→The experiment was conducted on a ${data.computer} with a ${data.monitor}. Stimuli were presented using ${data.software}. ${data.timingPrecision}. Responses were collected using ${data.inputDevices}. ${data.additionalEquipment}
   667→`;
   668→        
   669→        case 'procedure':
   670→          return `
   671→**Procedure**
   672→
   673→${data.overview}. ${data.trialStructure} ${data.timing} ${data.instructions} ${data.breaks}
   674→`;
   675→        
   676→        default:
   677→          return `[${section} section to be completed]`;
   678→      }
   679→    }
   680→  },
   681→  
   682→  // Additional templates for other formats
   683→  markdown: { /* Markdown formatting */ },
   684→  latex: { /* LaTeX formatting */ }
   685→};
   686→```
   687→
   688→#### GUI-012: Add Export Menu
   689→**Points: 2 | Priority: Medium**
   690→
   691→**Description:** Integrate data export and methods generation into Studio user interface.
   692→
   693→**Technical Requirements:**
   694→- Export menu with multiple format options
   695→- Progress indicators for large dataset exports
   696→- Preview functionality for methods sections
   697→- One-click export bundles (data + scripts + methods)
   698→- Export history and re-export capabilities
   699→
   700→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   701→- [ ] Export menu accessible from main Studio interface
   702→- [ ] Multiple export formats available with clear descriptions
   703→- [ ] Progress indicators show export status for large datasets
   704→- [ ] Methods section preview allows editing before export
   705→- [ ] Bundle export includes all necessary files for analysis
   706→- [ ] Export history allows re-downloading previous exports
   707→
   708→---
   709→
   710→### Epic: Documentation & Best Practices
   711→**Story Points: 3**
   712→
   713→#### DOC-003: Write "Literate Design" Guide
   714→**Points: 3 | Priority: Medium**
   715→
   716→**Description:** Create comprehensive documentation for reproducible experimental design using Psycho-Forge.
   717→
   718→**Technical Requirements:**
   719→- Best practices guide for experiment design
   720→- Code style guidelines for ForgeScript
   721→- Reproducibility checklist and validation
   722→- Example workflows and case studies
   723→- Integration with academic publishing requirements
   724→
   725→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   726→- [ ] Complete guide covers experiment design workflow
   727→- [ ] Code examples demonstrate best practices
   728→- [ ] Reproducibility checklist ensures experiment validity
   729→- [ ] Case studies show real-world applications
   730→- [ ] Documentation integrated into Hub and Studio help systems
   731→
   732→---
   733→
   734→## Definition of Done
   735→
   736→**For each ticket:**
   737→- [ ] Implementation with comprehensive unit tests (>90% coverage)
   738→- [ ] Export functionality tested with real experimental data
   739→- [ ] Generated scripts validated with statistical software
   740→- [ ] Methods sections reviewed for accuracy and completeness
   741→- [ ] **Gemini Pro audit passed**: User experience, academic accuracy, export quality
   742→- [ ] **O3 audit passed**: Data export integrity, script generation, statistical correctness
   743→
   744→**For sprint completion:**
   745→- [ ] Researchers can export analysis-ready data in multiple formats
   746→- [ ] Generated R and Python scripts successfully analyze exported data
   747→- [ ] Methods sections automatically generated with publication quality
   748→- [ ] Complete workflow from design to publication demonstrated
   749→- [ ] Documentation supports reproducible research practices
   750→
   751→---
   752→
   753→## Quality Gates & Audit Requirements
   754→
   755→### Full-Cycle Workflow Testing
   756→- [ ] End-to-end testing from experiment design to data analysis
   757→- [ ] Generated scripts tested with real experimental datasets
   758→- [ ] Methods sections reviewed by academic researchers
   759→- [ ] Export formats validated with external tools (BIDS validator, R, Python)
   760→- [ ] Publication workflow tested with journal submission requirements
   761→
   762→---
   763→
   764→## Risk Mitigation
   765→
   766→**Academic Risks:**
   767→- **Methods accuracy**: Comprehensive review by experimental psychology experts
   768→- **Statistical validity**: Validation of generated analysis scripts with known datasets
   769→- **Reproducibility**: Extensive testing of export and re-import workflows
   770→
   771→**Technical Risks:**
   772→- **Export performance**: Optimization for large datasets with progress indicators
   773→- **Script compatibility**: Testing across multiple R and Python versions
   774→
   775→---
   776→
   777→*Previous: [Sprint 7: Ecosystem Foundations](sprint7.md) | Next: [Sprint 9: Polish & Alpha Prep](sprint9.md)*

content.text:
After reviewing all the sprint files (sprint1.md through sprint10.md, excluding sprint6.md which you mentioned was already cleaned), I found **no non-technical tasks** related to hiring, recruitment, team building, academic partnerships, or business development that don't involve writing code or implementing technical features.

## Summary of Findings

All tasks across the sprint files are **purely technical** and focus on:

1. **Software Development**: Building runtime cores, compilers, GUI components, APIs, etc.
2. **Technical Implementation**: Device drivers, databases, security systems, monitoring
3. **Engineering Tasks**: Performance optimization, testing, deployment, infrastructure
4. **Product Development**: User interfaces, features, documentation, technical validation

## What I Did NOT Find

- No hiring or recruitment tasks
- No team building activities  
- No business development initiatives
- No academic partnership building (beyond technical integration)
- No marketing campaigns (except for 3 technical demo videos in Sprint 9)
- No fundraising activities
- No organizational development tasks

## Technical Marketing Tasks Found

The only tasks that could be considered "business-related" are actually technical in nature:

- **Sprint 9, MKT-001**: "Record 3 'Paradigm-in-a-Tweet' Videos" - These are technical demonstration videos showing software capabilities
- **Sprint 10, CSM-001**: "Onboard 3 Founding Labs" - This is customer success management for technical alpha users

Even these tasks are focused on demonstrating technical capabilities and supporting users with technical integration, rather than traditional business development activities.

## Conclusion

All sprint files contain only technical tasks related to building, testing, deploying, and supporting the Psycho-Forge platform. There are no non-technical business, hiring, or organizational tasks that need to be removed.

message.content:
I just want to make sure all the technical element from our origianl draft are preset in some form; please evaluate this. this was the origianl draft form that we iterated on: 
Excellent. The next logical step is to break this comprehensive vision into an actionable, sprint-by-sprint execution plan. This plan is designed for an agile team, focusing on delivering demonstrable value in two-week increments. Each sprint has a clear goal, and the tickets are granular enough to be assigned and completed within that timeframe.
This roadmap assumes a core team of ~4 FTEs (Lead/Architect, Kernel Dev, Frontend/Studio Dev, UX/QA) starting in Q3 2025.
Psycho-Forge: 10-Sprint Execution Plan (Initial 5 Months)
Sprint 0: The Foundation (Weeks 1-2)
Goal: Establish the core project infrastructure and development environment.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
INF-001    Stand up Monorepo (Turborepo + pnpm)    As a dev, I need a version-controlled monorepo so that all packages (kernel, dsl, studio) can be managed cohesively.    3
INF-002    Configure CI/CD Pipeline (GitHub Actions)    As a dev, I need an automated CI pipeline that runs linting, typing, and unit tests on every push.    5
DSL-001    Define Initial AST Schema (Zod)    As a dev, I need the basic Zod schemas for Timeline, Trial, and Stimulus nodes to unblock kernel and compiler work.    5
DOC-001    Draft FEP-0000: Governance Model    As the lead, I need to formalize the FEP process document to establish clear rules for future contributions.    3
ARC-001    Spike: Kernel-to-Renderer Proof of Concept    As a kernel dev, I need a minimal "hello world" that renders a single Pixi.js shape controlled by a basic runtime scheduler.    8
Sprint 1: The Runtime Kernel & a Runnable Trial (Weeks 3-4)
Goal: Have a single, runnable trial with millisecond-accurate timing.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
KER-001    Implement High-Precision Clock    As a kernel dev, I need a clock module using performance.now() with requestAnimationFrame for stable event loops.    5
KER-002    Implement Hierarchical Scheduler (Timeline)    As a kernel dev, I need a scheduler that can execute a sequence of events defined in a simple AST.    8
DSL-002    Implement FZB (Protobuf) Serialization    As a dev, I need to serialize the Zod-defined AST to and from the canonical FZB format.    5
KER-003    Implement FixationCross & KeyPress Components    As a dev, I need two basic components to create a simple RT task.    5
TST-001    Create Initial Timing Benchmark Harness    As a QA dev, I need a script that runs a simple trial 1000 times and logs onset/offset timing data.    5
Sprint 2: The ForgeScript Compiler & the First Full Experiment (Weeks 5-6)
Goal: Compile a complete ForgeScript-TS file into a runnable FZB.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
DSL-003    Build TS Parser (ts-morph) & Compiler Stub    As a dev, I need a compiler that can parse a .ts file and output a basic, unvalidated AST.    8
DSL-004    Implement DSL Type System & Validation    As a dev, I need the compiler to use Zod schemas to validate the parsed AST and provide typed IntelliSense data.    8
EGS-001    Port simple_rt Example to ForgeScript    As a dev, I need to write the Simple Reaction Time task in the new DSL to test the compiler end-to-end.    3
KER-004    Implement Basic Data Sink API    As a kernel dev, I need the runtime to collect trial data (RT, response) and store it in a simple in-memory array.    5
DOC-002    Publish Benchmark White-Paper v0.1    As the lead, I need to publish the initial timing results from TST-001 to establish credibility.    2
Sprint 3: The Mirror-Canvas GUI - The "View" (Weeks 7-8)
Goal: Visually render a static experiment AST in Forge Studio.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
GUI-001    Set up SvelteKit Project for Forge Studio    As a frontend dev, I need the basic SvelteKit application structure with Vite HMR configured.    3
GUI-002    Implement Visual Timeline Pane (Read-Only)    As a frontend dev, I need a component that can take an FZB file and render it as a visual graph of blocks and trials.    8
GUI-003    Implement Code Pane (Monaco Editor)    As a frontend dev, I need to embed the Monaco editor and load a ForgeScript file into it.    5
GUI-004    Implement Properties Inspector (Read-Only)    As a frontend dev, I need a panel that displays the parameters of a selected visual block.    5
ARC-002    Spike: CRDT State Management Proof of Concept    As the lead, I need to prove that a change in an Automerge document can trigger a reactive update in a Svelte component.    5
Sprint 4: Making the Mirror-Canvas "Live" (Weeks 9-10)
Goal: Achieve full, bidirectional, real-time sync between the code and visual panes.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
GUI-005    Integrate Automerge as Central State Store    As a frontend dev, I need to refactor the Studio to use an Automerge document in a Web Worker as the single source of truth.    8
GUI-006    Implement Visual-to-AST Binding    As a frontend dev, I need to make dragging a block on the timeline generate and apply a change to the Automerge document.    8
GUI-007    Implement AST-to-Code Binding    As a dev, I need to integrate the incremental compiler so that changes to the Automerge AST are reflected as text patches in the Monaco editor.    8
GUI-008    Implement "Self-Healing" on Syntax Error    As a frontend dev, I need the code pane to show compiler errors as diagnostics without crashing the sync.    3
Sprint 5: Simulation & Intelligence (Weeks 11-12)
Goal: Bring the Time-Travel Simulator and AI-assist online in their most basic forms.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
SIM-001    Package @psyforge/headless (WASM)    As a kernel dev, I need to compile the runtime kernel into a WASM package that can run in a Web Worker.    8
GUI-009    Implement Time-Travel Scrubber UI & Worker    As a frontend dev, I need a UI scrubber that controls the headless simulator and receives state events to drive the preview.    8
AI-001    Implement AI Command Palette Backend Hook    As a dev, I need to create a secure API endpoint that takes a natural language prompt and returns a validated AST patch from an LLM.    5
GUI-010    Integrate AI Command Palette in Studio    As a frontend dev, I need a command palette UI (Cmd+K) that calls the AI backend and applies the returned patch.    5
TST-002    Add Fuzz Testing to CI Pipeline    As a QA dev, I need a CI step that generates and compiles 10,000 random DSL programs to catch compiler edge cases.    3
Sprint 6: The Ecosystem - Part 1 (Recruitment & Balancing) (Weeks 13-14)
Goal: Implement the core server-side logic for recruitment and counterbalancing.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
SRV-001    Build ForgeRouter Service (Fastify)    As a kernel dev, I need a public-facing router that can handle incoming participant links.    5
SRV-002    Implement Global State DB & Transactional Logic    As a kernel dev, I need a Postgres DB and logic for atomically updating let_global counters.    8
DSL-005    Implement latinSquare & counter Primitives    As a dev, I need the compiler to recognize and correctly specify these server-side primitives in the FZB.    5
URF-001    Implement @psyforge/recruit-bridge for Prolific    As a dev, I need a backend library that can create a study on Prolific via their API.    8
GUI-011    Build Basic Recruitment Tab UI    As a frontend dev, I need a UI to connect a Prolific account and set N/Pay, which calls the URF API on "Launch".    3
Sprint 7: The Ecosystem - Part 2 (Hub & DAL) (Weeks 15-16)
Goal: Establish the foundations for community collaboration and hardware integration.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
HUB-001    Set up Gitea Instance for Forge Hub    As the lead, I need a self-hosted Git server to act as the backend for the paradigm registry.    3
HUB-002    Implement Hub API (Read-Only) & Studio Integration    As a dev, I need an API to list paradigms and a "New from Hub" dialog in the Studio that consumes it.    8
DAL-001    Finalize DAL Interfaces (ResponseDevice, etc.)    As a kernel dev, I need to finalize the core TypeScript interfaces for the Device Abstraction Layer.    3
DAL-002    Implement Virtual Device Lab & Keyboard Driver    As a dev, I need the Studio panel for virtual devices and a "Virtual Keyboard" driver for testing.    8
EGS-002    Begin "100-in-100" Sprint: Port 10 Paradigms    As a dev, I need to port the first 10 classic tasks (Stroop, Flanker, etc.) to seed the Hub.    5
Sprint 8: The Full-Cycle Workflow - Part 1 (Methods & Analysis) (Weeks 17-18)
Goal: Close the loop from design to reporting.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
MSG-001    Implement Methods Section Generator (AST -> Markdown)    As a dev, I need a module that traverses a FZB and outputs a Markdown description of the design.    8
ARE-001    Implement Analysis-Ready Export (FZB -> Tidy CSV)    As a dev, I need a module that takes raw data and an FZB to produce a clean, factor-labeled CSV.    8
ARE-002    Implement R Script Code Generation    As a dev, I need to extend the ARE to generate a basic analysis.R script that loads the tidy data and creates a ggplot.    5
GUI-012    Add "Export" Menu with MSG/ARE Options    As a frontend dev, I need to add UI buttons to trigger the MSG and ARE pipelines.    3
DOC-003    Draft "Literate Design" Documentation    As the lead, I need to write a guide explaining how to use docstrings and the MSG for preregistration.    2
Sprint 9: Polish, Onboarding & Alpha Prep (Weeks 19-20)
Goal: Refine the user experience and prepare for the first external testers.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
GUI-013    Implement RAPD Framework (Role-Adaptive UI)    As a frontend dev, I need to implement the logic for showing/hiding UI elements based on the user's selected role.    8
GUI-014    Build "First-Run Orbit" Ghost Demo    As a frontend dev, I need to create the animated onboarding tour that plays on first launch.    5
DOC-004    Create Interactive "Hello, World" Tutorial    As a dev, I need to build the first interactive tutorial using the "code coach" framework.    8
INF-003    Set up Alpha Deployment Environment    As a dev, I need to configure the cloud infrastructure to host the Studio and backend services for the founding consortium.    5
MKT-001    Record First 3 "Paradigm-in-a-Tweet" Videos    As the lead, I need to produce the initial set of marketing videos for the GTM campaign.    3
50.6s
please continue with finihing sprint 9 and providing sprint 10
 :: original proposal:: Understood. The existing proposal is strategically sound but requires a deep, technical "implementation-level" expansion to serve as a true blueprint for the engineering team. This document provides that level of detail for every major technical element discussed, synthesizing all prior strategic decisions into a concrete technical specification.

This is a comprehensive, multi-part technical addendum designed to guide the implementation of the Psycho-Forge platform.

***

## Psycho-Forge: Technical Implementation Specification

**Version:** 1.0  
**Status:** Internal Engineering Blueprint

### **Part 1: The ForgeScript Compiler & AST**

The entire Psycho-Forge ecosystem is built upon a single source of truth: the **Forge Binary Abstract Syntax Tree (FZB)**. The ForgeScript language (both its TS and JSON flavors) is the human-readable frontend for this canonical model.

#### **1.1. Core Compiler Architecture**

*   **Parser:** We will use `ts-morph` to parse ForgeScript-TS files. Its ability to navigate and manipulate the TypeScript AST is crucial for our complex analysis and macro expansion. For ForgeScript-Lite (JSON), a simple JSON parser coupled with a Zod validator is sufficient.
*   **AST Schema (Zod):** The canonical AST structure will be defined using Zod schemas. This provides a single source of truth for:
    *   TypeScript type definitions, enabling IDE IntelliSense.
    *   Runtime validation for both JSON and compiled TS code.
    *   Automatic generation of JSON Schema for interoperability.
*   **Canonical Representation (FZB):** The in-memory AST, once validated, will be serialized to a binary format using Protocol Buffers (`protobuf-js`). This **Forge Binary AST (FZB)** is the version-pinned, canonical representation of an experiment. It is what gets stored, version-controlled, and passed to all other system components (Runtime, Studio, ARE, MSG).
*   **Incremental Compiler:** For the Studio's live-sync feature, the compiler will operate incrementally. It will maintain an in-memory AST and use patch-diffing algorithms (derived from the CRDT changes) to re-validate and re-compile only the affected sub-trees, enabling sub-50ms feedback loops.

This plan is designed for an agile team, focusing on delivering demonstrable value in two-week increments. Psycho-Forge: 10-Sprint Execution Plan (Initial 5 Months)
Sprint 0: The Foundation (Weeks 1-2)
Goal: Establish the core project infrastructure and development environment.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
INF-001    Stand up Monorepo (Turborepo + pnpm)    As a dev, I need a version-controlled monorepo so that all packages (kernel, dsl, studio) can be managed cohesively.    3
INF-002    Configure CI/CD Pipeline (GitHub Actions)    As a dev, I need an automated CI pipeline that runs linting, typing, and unit tests on every push.    5
DSL-001    Define Initial AST Schema (Zod)    As a dev, I need the basic Zod schemas for Timeline, Trial, and Stimulus nodes to unblock kernel and compiler work.    5
DOC-001    Draft FEP-0000: Governance Model    As the lead, I need to formalize the FEP process document to establish clear rules for future contributions.    3
ARC-001    Spike: Kernel-to-Renderer Proof of Concept    As a kernel dev, I need a minimal "hello world" that renders a single Pixi.js shape controlled by a basic runtime scheduler.    8
Sprint 1: The Runtime Kernel & a Runnable Trial (Weeks 3-4)
Goal: Have a single, runnable trial with millisecond-accurate timing.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
KER-001    Implement High-Precision Clock    As a kernel dev, I need a clock module using performance.now() with requestAnimationFrame for stable event loops.    5
KER-002    Implement Hierarchical Scheduler (Timeline)    As a kernel dev, I need a scheduler that can execute a sequence of events defined in a simple AST.    8
DSL-002    Implement FZB (Protobuf) Serialization    As a dev, I need to serialize the Zod-defined AST to and from the canonical FZB format.    5
KER-003    Implement FixationCross & KeyPress Components    As a dev, I need two basic components to create a simple RT task.    5
TST-001    Create Initial Timing Benchmark Harness    As a QA dev, I need a script that runs a simple trial 1000 times and logs onset/offset timing data.    5
Sprint 2: The ForgeScript Compiler & the First Full Experiment (Weeks 5-6)
Goal: Compile a complete ForgeScript-TS file into a runnable FZB.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
DSL-003    Build TS Parser (ts-morph) & Compiler Stub    As a dev, I need a compiler that can parse a .ts file and output a basic, unvalidated AST.    8
DSL-004    Implement DSL Type System & Validation    As a dev, I need the compiler to use Zod schemas to validate the parsed AST and provide typed IntelliSense data.    8
EGS-001    Port simple_rt Example to ForgeScript    As a dev, I need to write the Simple Reaction Time task in the new DSL to test the compiler end-to-end.    3
KER-004    Implement Basic Data Sink API    As a kernel dev, I need the runtime to collect trial data (RT, response) and store it in a simple in-memory array.    5
DOC-002    Publish Benchmark White-Paper v0.1    As the lead, I need to publish the initial timing results from TST-001 to establish credibility.    2
Sprint 3: The Mirror-Canvas GUI - The "View" (Weeks 7-8)
Goal: Visually render a static experiment AST in Forge Studio.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
GUI-001    Set up SvelteKit Project for Forge Studio    As a frontend dev, I need the basic SvelteKit application structure with Vite HMR configured.    3
GUI-002    Implement Visual Timeline Pane (Read-Only)    As a frontend dev, I need a component that can take an FZB file and render it as a visual graph of blocks and trials.    8
GUI-003    Implement Code Pane (Monaco Editor)    As a frontend dev, I need to embed the Monaco editor and load a ForgeScript file into it.    5
GUI-004    Implement Properties Inspector (Read-Only)    As a frontend dev, I need a panel that displays the parameters of a selected visual block.    5
ARC-002    Spike: CRDT State Management Proof of Concept    As the lead, I need to prove that a change in an Automerge document can trigger a reactive update in a Svelte component.    5
Sprint 4: Making the Mirror-Canvas "Live" (Weeks 9-10)
Goal: Achieve full, bidirectional, real-time sync between the code and visual panes.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
GUI-005    Integrate Automerge as Central State Store    As a frontend dev, I need to refactor the Studio to use an Automerge document in a Web Worker as the single source of truth.    8
GUI-006    Implement Visual-to-AST Binding    As a frontend dev, I need to make dragging a block on the timeline generate and apply a change to the Automerge document.    8
GUI-007    Implement AST-to-Code Binding    As a dev, I need to integrate the incremental compiler so that changes to the Automerge AST are reflected as text patches in the Monaco editor.    8
GUI-008    Implement "Self-Healing" on Syntax Error    As a frontend dev, I need the code pane to show compiler errors as diagnostics without crashing the sync.    3
Sprint 5: Simulation & Intelligence (Weeks 11-12)
Goal: Bring the Time-Travel Simulator and AI-assist online in their most basic forms.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
SIM-001    Package @psyforge/headless (WASM)    As a kernel dev, I need to compile the runtime kernel into a WASM package that can run in a Web Worker.    8
GUI-009    Implement Time-Travel Scrubber UI & Worker    As a frontend dev, I need a UI scrubber that controls the headless simulator and receives state events to drive the preview.    8
AI-001    Implement AI Command Palette Backend Hook    As a dev, I need to create a secure API endpoint that takes a natural language prompt and returns a validated AST patch from an LLM.    5
GUI-010    Integrate AI Command Palette in Studio    As a frontend dev, I need a command palette UI (Cmd+K) that calls the AI backend and applies the returned patch.    5
TST-002    Add Fuzz Testing to CI Pipeline    As a QA dev, I need a CI step that generates and compiles 10,000 random DSL programs to catch compiler edge cases.    3
Sprint 6: The Ecosystem - Part 1 (Recruitment & Balancing) (Weeks 13-14)
Goal: Implement the core server-side logic for recruitment and counterbalancing.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
SRV-001    Build ForgeRouter Service (Fastify)    As a kernel dev, I need a public-facing router that can handle incoming participant links.    5
SRV-002    Implement Global State DB & Transactional Logic    As a kernel dev, I need a Postgres DB and logic for atomically updating let_global counters.    8
DSL-005    Implement latinSquare & counter Primitives    As a dev, I need the compiler to recognize and correctly specify these server-side primitives in the FZB.    5
URF-001    Implement @psyforge/recruit-bridge for Prolific    As a dev, I need a backend library that can create a study on Prolific via their API.    8
GUI-011    Build Basic Recruitment Tab UI    As a frontend dev, I need a UI to connect a Prolific account and set N/Pay, which calls the URF API on "Launch".    3
Sprint 7: The Ecosystem - Part 2 (Hub & DAL) (Weeks 15-16)
Goal: Establish the foundations for community collaboration and hardware integration.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
HUB-001    Set up Gitea Instance for Forge Hub    As the lead, I need a self-hosted Git server to act as the backend for the paradigm registry.    3
HUB-002    Implement Hub API (Read-Only) & Studio Integration    As a dev, I need an API to list paradigms and a "New from Hub" dialog in the Studio that consumes it.    8
DAL-001    Finalize DAL Interfaces (ResponseDevice, etc.)    As a kernel dev, I need to finalize the core TypeScript interfaces for the Device Abstraction Layer.    3
DAL-002    Implement Virtual Device Lab & Keyboard Driver    As a dev, I need the Studio panel for virtual devices and a "Virtual Keyboard" driver for testing.    8
EGS-002    Begin "100-in-100" Sprint: Port 10 Paradigms    As a dev, I need to port the first 10 classic tasks (Stroop, Flanker, etc.) to seed the Hub.    5
Sprint 8: The Full-Cycle Workflow - Part 1 (Methods & Analysis) (Weeks 17-18)
Goal: Close the loop from design to reporting.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
MSG-001    Implement Methods Section Generator (AST -> Markdown)    As a dev, I need a module that traverses a FZB and outputs a Markdown description of the design.    8
ARE-001    Implement Analysis-Ready Export (FZB -> Tidy CSV)    As a dev, I need a module that takes raw data and an FZB to produce a clean, factor-labeled CSV.    8
ARE-002    Implement R Script Code Generation    As a dev, I need to extend the ARE to generate a basic analysis.R script that loads the tidy data and creates a ggplot.    5
GUI-012    Add "Export" Menu with MSG/ARE Options    As a frontend dev, I need to add UI buttons to trigger the MSG and ARE pipelines.    3
DOC-003    Draft "Literate Design" Documentation    As the lead, I need to write a guide explaining how to use docstrings and the MSG for preregistration.    2
Sprint 9: Polish, Onboarding & Alpha Prep (Weeks 19-20)
Goal: Refine the user experience and prepare for the first external testers.
Ticket ID    Title    Story    Est. Points
GUI-013    Implement RAPD Framework (Role-Adaptive UI)    As a frontend dev, I need to implement the logic for showing/hiding UI elements based on the user's selected role.    8
GUI-014    Build "First-Run Orbit" Ghost Demo    As a frontend dev, I need to create the animated onboarding tour that plays on first launch.    5
DOC-004    Create Interactive "Hello, World" Tutorial    As a dev, I need to build the first interactive tutorial using the "code coach" framework.    8
INF-003    Set up Alpha Deployment Environment    As a dev, I need to configure the cloud infrastructure to host the Studio and backend services for the founding consortium.    5
MKT-001    Record First 3 "Paradigm-in-a-Tweet" Videos    As the lead, I need to produce the initial set of marketing videos for the GTM campaign.    3

 :: original proposal:: -declared variable for the duration of its timeline. The size of the buffer is determined statically by the maximum `lag` depth requested.
    *   `counter()` is a server-side primitive. The `let_global` AST node flags it for execution by the Cloud/Box backend's transactional state manager.
*   **Spec-First Macros (`@factorial`)**:
    *   These are implemented as AST-to-AST transforms that run at compile-time. The `@factorial` macro receives its parameters, generates the corresponding balanced `timeline` and `trial` nodes, and replaces itself in the AST before the main validation pass. This ensures macros are hygienic and their output is fully analyzable.
*   **Formal Checks (`require power()`)**:
    *   This is another compile-time AST transform. The `require` node triggers a call to a sandboxed WebAssembly module containing a port of the `pwr` R package. The module receives the trial count, effect size, and alpha from the AST, and returns a power value. If the value is below the required threshold, the compiler emits a build error.

### **Part 2: Forge Studio & The Mirror-Canvas**

The Mirror-Canvas is not two separate applications but a single SvelteKit application rendering two different views of the same underlying CRDT-managed state.

#### **2.1. Bidirectional Sync Engine**

1.  **State Model (Automerge):** The entire experiment state (the FZB AST) is held in an Automerge document within a Web Worker.
2.  **Visual Canvas (Svelte + PixiJS):** The left pane is a Svelte component that subscribes to the Automerge document. When the AST changes, Svelte's reactivity triggers a re-render of the visual timeline graph. Drag-and-drop actions don't manipulate the DOM directly; they generate "change functions" that are applied to the Automerge document (e.g., `(doc) => doc.timelines[0].trials.move(3, 5)`).
3.  **Code Pane (Monaco Editor):** The right pane is an instance of the Monaco Editor (the engine behind VS Code). It also subscribes to the Automerge document. When the document changes, a patch is generated and applied to the editor's text model. When the user types, the editor's `onChange` event triggers a call to the incremental compiler. If it passes, a change function is applied back to the Automerge document.
4.  **Self-Healing:** If the incremental compiler fails, the Automerge document is *not* updated. Instead, the compiler's error message (with line/column info) is passed to the Monaco editor to render as a diagnostic squiggle. The AI-assist module receives the error and the code context to generate a fix suggestion.

#### **2.2. Embedded Intelligence & Diagnostics**

*   **AI Command Palette:**
    *   Input (e.g., "Add 20 stroop trials") is sent to a backend LLM API along with the current AST schema and context.
    *   The LLM is prompted to return not just code, but a structured JSON object representing the patch to be applied to the AST.
    *   This patch is validated against the Zod schema before being applied, preventing hallucinated or invalid code.
*   **Constraint & Latency Visualizers:**
    *   These are Svelte components that also subscribe to the Automerge AST.
    *   The **Latency Meter** continuously sends the current AST to the headless runtime in a separate Web Worker. The runtime's scheduler calculates a predicted execution path and returns a telemetry object (e.g., `{max_frame_load: 12.5, drop_risk: 'amber'}`).
    *   The **Constraint Visualizer** runs the balancing solver on the AST and renders the resulting matrix as a heatmap.

#### **2.3. Time-Travel Simulator**

*   This feature re-uses the `@psyforge/headless` package, compiled to WASM and running inside a Web Worker.
*   The simulator is initialized with the current FZB AST and a set of "Virtual Device" drivers.
*   The scrubber UI controls a `virtual_time` variable. As the user scrubs, the simulator runs the experiment up to that virtual timestamp.
*   It emits a continuous stream of state events (e.g., `{time: 500.1, event: 'stim_show', stim: 'A.png'}` or `{time: 850.3, event: 'device_response', device: 'Cedrus', key: 2}`).
*   The Studio's visual canvas and code pane subscribe to this event stream and highlight the corresponding elements.

### **Part 3: Universal Recruitment Fabric (URF)**

The URF abstracts recruitment logistics into a declarative API, managed by the Psycho-Forge Cloud/Box backend.

#### **3.1. Provider-Meta Registry**

*   A service that periodically scrapes the public APIs and documentation of supported providers (Prolific, MTurk, etc.).
*   It stores a versioned JSON schema for each provider's prescreening attributes (e.g., `{provider: 'prolific', version: '2025-07-20', attributes: [{id: '540d9...', name: 'First Language', type: 'enum', values: [...]}]}`).
*   Forge Studio fetches this registry on load to dynamically build the "Attribute Chips" UI, ensuring it's always up-to-date.

#### **3.2. ForgeRouter & Session Initiation**

1.  A participant clicks the study link from Prolific, which points to `https://cloud.psyforge.org/router/STUDY_ID?PROLIFIC_PID=...`.
2.  The **ForgeRouter** (a Fastify service) receives the request.
3.  It initiates a **database transaction** with `SERIALIZABLE` isolation.
4.  Inside the transaction, it reads the study's `let_global` rules from the FZB spec.
5.  It executes the `counter()` or `latinSquare()` assignments, atomically incrementing the global counters and locking the assigned condition for this participant.
6.  It generates a short-lived **JSON Web Token (JWT)** containing the `participant_id`, `study_version`, and the assigned `condition_levels`.
7.  The router responds with a 302 redirect to the experiment runner URL, with the JWT included.

#### **3.3. `@psyforge/recruit-bridge`**

*   A backend TypeScript library containing provider-specific "driver" classes.
*   Each driver implements a standard interface: `createStudy(spec)`, `approveSubmission(id)`, `bonusPayment(id, amount)`.
*   The URF uses these drivers to translate the declarative `recruitment` block from the DSL into concrete API calls.

### **Part 4: Device Abstraction Layer (DAL) & Virtual Device Lab**

This subsystem makes hardware integration a simple software task.

#### **4.1. DAL API**

The DAL is defined by a small set of TypeScript interfaces in the `@psyforge/dal` package.

```typescript
// Example for a response device
interface ResponseDevice {
  readonly deviceId: string;
  readonly deviceName: string;
  connect(): Promise<void>;
  // Emits events with { key: number, port: number, timestamp: DOMHighResTimeStamp }
  on(event: 'response', listener: (data: any) => void): this;
  disconnect(): Promise<void>;
}
```

#### **4.2. Virtual Device Lab Implementation**

*   The Virtual Device Lab in Forge Studio is a Svelte component that presents a UI for each known device type.
*   It contains "virtual drivers" that implement the same DAL interfaces.
*   The "Sim Cedrus Box" virtual driver, for instance, has a UI with buttons. Clicking a button calls the `emit('response', ...)` method, sending a simulated event into the running experiment.
*   The Time-Travel Simulator uses these virtual drivers in non-interactive mode, generating events based on a pre-defined statistical model (e.g., a Lognormal RT distribution).

#### **4.3. Headless Simulation (`@psyforge/headless`)**

*   This package bundles the core runtime kernel (compiled to WASM for performance), the DAL, and all virtual device drivers.
*   It exposes a simple API that takes an FZB spec and a cognitive model (a user-provided function that returns a response given a stimulus).
*   It runs the entire experiment deterministically in memory at maximum speed, bypassing all real-time waits, and returns the complete data output as a structured array or DataFrame, making it perfect for model fitting and large-scale simulation.

This technical specification provides the concrete engineering details needed to build the Psycho-Forge platform as envisioned. ## Project Proposal: Psycho-Forge

**A Full-Cycle, Intelligent Operating System for Experimental Science**

**Version:** Internal Candidate Release 1.1  
**Version Date:** July 2025  
**Status:** Confidential, for Founding Consortium Review

### 1. Executive Summary

The current landscape of software for psychological and behavioral research is fragmented and outdated. Existing tools force researchers into a high-friction workflow, present significant risks to scientific rigor, and fail to support modern collaborative science.

**Psycho-Forge is the definitive solution.** It is not an incremental improvement but a paradigm shift—a full-cycle, intelligent operating system for experimental science designed to obliterate the status quo. Our platform achieves **verifiable ≤ 2 ms onset jitter** on 144 Hz monitors (see Appendix C) and has demonstrated a **10× faster design cycle** in pilot labs.

Our platform is built on three revolutionary pillars:

1.  **A Declarative, Type-Safe Language (ForgeScript):** We replace brittle, imperative JavaScript with a concise, statically-analyzable language for describing experiments, guaranteeing correctness and making complex designs trivially easy to express.

2.  **The Mirror-Canvas GUI (Forge Studio):** A groundbreaking visual builder with a live, self-healing, bidirectional link to the ForgeScript code. It features an AI-assisted command palette, a time-travel simulator for pre-emptive debugging, and live diagnostic overlays.

3.  **An Integrated Scientific Ecosystem:** Psycho-Forge is more than a tool; it's an end-to-end workflow. It automates participant recruitment, generates publication-ready methods sections and analysis scripts, and fosters collaboration through a version-controlled "GitHub for Experiments."

### 2. The Opportunity

Modern behavioral research demands precision, reproducibility, and speed. The current software landscape fails on all three counts, creating a tooling crisis. With an estimated **~45,000 active behavioral science labs globally** and a Total Addressable Market (TAM) for tooling and participant fees exceeding **$250M annually**, there is a clear and substantial market for a superior solution. Psycho-Forge seizes this opportunity by treating experiment design as a formal engineering discipline.

### 3. Architecture

Our architecture is built on a clean separation of concerns, ensuring long-term stability and future-proof innovation.

#### 3.1. High-level Architecture

```
             ┌───────────────── Forge Studio (GUI) ──────────────────┐
             │    SvelteKit + Vite + Monaco Editor + CRDT Engine     │
             └──────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                                    ▼ (Reads/Writes)
                      ┌──────────  ForgeScript AST  ───────────┐
                      │(Canonical, Zod-validated, Forge Binary AST = FZB)│
                      └──────────────────────┬─────────────────┘
                                             ▼ (Compiles to)
┌───────────────────────── Runtime Kernel (WASM-Ready) ──────────────────────────┐
│  • Hierarchical Scheduler    • Renderer (PixiJS/WebGL)   • Input DAL          │
│  • Data Sink API             • Precision Clock             • Plugin Host        │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### 3.2. Technology Stack & Security

| Component | Technology | Rationale |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Language** | **TypeScript** | Type safety, modern syntax, and a robust ecosystem for a reliable platform. |
| **GUI Framework** | **SvelteKit + Vite** | High-performance, boilerplate-free code and near-instantaneous development. |
| **Graphics Engine** | **Pixi.js v6+** | High-performance WebGL2 renderer enabling sub-millisecond timing fidelity. |
| **DSL Schema** | **Zod** | TypeScript-first schema declaration and validation for the canonical AST. |
| **Real-time Sync** | **CRDTs (Automerge)** | Guarantees robust, error-free multi-user collaboration in the Studio. |
| **Backend & Security**| **Fastify, Keycloak (OAuth2/PKCE)** | Modern, secure stack for the managed service and local appliance. Secures all API interactions. |
| **CSP & SRI** | **Automated Header Generation** | Studio templates will include strict Content-Security-Policy and Sub-Resource Integrity hashes to pass university IT security reviews. |

### 4. ForgeScript DSL

ForgeScript is the canonical heart of the system. It is a purely declarative, typed, and composable language for defining experiments.

*   **Key Features:** Structural Typing, Declarative Side-Effects, First-Class Units, Constraint Solvers, Timeline Algebra, Reactive State, and Embedded Formal Checks (`require power()`).
*   **Precision:** The compiler will warn if `duration` literals are not integer multiples of the target display's frame duration (e.g., `17ms` at 60Hz).
*   **Data Schema:** The data export schema (column names, units) will be versioned and locked early in development to ensure backward compatibility for analysis pipelines.

### 5. Forge Studio: The Mirror-Canvas GUI

Forge Studio is the revolutionary visual interface to the Psycho-Forge ecosystem. Its core is the **Mirror-Canvas**, a live, self-healing, dual-pane view that unifies visual design and code.

*   **Key Capabilities:** Dual-Pane "Self-Healing" View, AI-Assisted Design, Time-Travel Simulator, Live Diagnostic Overlays, and Live Co-Design & Review.
*   **Performance:** A strict performance budget (e.g., <100MB RAM for a standard study) will be tracked via automated Lighthouse tests in CI to ensure the Studio remains responsive.
*   **Accessibility:** All GUI components will be developed to meet WCAG 2.2 AA standards for color-contrast and keyboard navigation from the start.

### 6. The Psycho-Forge Ecosystem

Psycho-Forge extends beyond the authoring tool to solve frictions across the entire research lifecycle.

*   **Technical Proof:** Publicly available benchmark suite and **Precision Validation Matrix** showing timing fidelity across OS/Browser/GPU combinations. The kernel will detect and warn about OS-level timer throttling.
*   **Paradigm & Asset Hub:** A version-controlled "GitHub for Experiments and Stimuli." A **"100-in-100" Sprint** will populate the Hub with 100+ classic paradigms at launch.
*   **Hardware Integration:** A **Device Abstraction Layer (DAL)** and SDK, advised by a council of hardware vendors, with certified drivers.
*   **Data Interoperability:** **Forge Connectors** for one-line data ingestion into R, Python (MNE), and MATLAB (EEGLAB), plus BIDS-formatter export.
*   **Lifecycle Automation:** Includes the **Analysis-Ready Export (ARE)**, **Methods Section Generator (MSG)**, and **Universal Recruitment Fabric (URF)**.
*   **Observability:** A full telemetry pipeline (Sentry, Prometheus) will be implemented early to instrument Studio errors, kernel exceptions, and URF failures for rapid diagnostics during alpha testing.

### 7. Enhanced Roadmap & Milestones (Revised)

*This roadmap includes newly identified technical prerequisites and has been adjusted for a realistic load distribution.*

| Quarter | Milestone | Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Q4 2025** | **Benchmarks & Validation Matrix Public Release**; 5 founding labs replicate. | Builds trust before Series A. |
| **Q2 2026** | **Cloud GA; "100-in-100" Paradigm Library Complete**; URF live. | Ensures Hub traction and a complete solution at public launch. |
| **Q4 2026** | **First Certified Hardware Drivers (Cedrus, Tobii)**; SOC 2 Type II audit begins. | Opens psychophys & eye-tracking market; unblocks enterprise. |
| **Q1 2027** | **DAL v1.0 GA**. | Signals maturity of hardware ecosystem. |
| **Q2 2027** | **WebGPU + Mobile Timing Certification**; WebXR module (beta). | Expands TAM to field, smartphone, and VR studies. |
| **Q3 2027** | **SOC 2 Type II report; HIPAA BA program live**. | Unblocks clinical and enterprise health customers. |

### 8. Go-to-Market: "Paradigm-in-a-Tweet"

Our launch will be a viral campaign featuring 30-second videos of the Mirror-Canvas building, validating, and launching complex experiments in moments. This visceral proof of power will be amplified through targeted pre-briefs with key academic influencers and a live "10-minute N-back challenge" against competitors.

### 9. Governance & Licensing

*   **Governance:** Psycho-Forge will evolve via **ForgeScript Enhancement Proposals (FEPs)**—a public RFC process.
*   **Licensing:** The core compiler, runtime, and Hub client will be licensed under **Apache-2.0**. Studio GUI & Cloud services will be under a commercial license with academic-friendly tiers. All repos will use SPDX identifiers and a CLA bot for contributions.

### 10. Financial Overview

*   **24-Month Burn:** Estimated at **USD $3.5M** to support a core team of 8 FTEs plus infrastructure costs.
*   **Revenue Streams:** Primary revenue will be generated from tiered Cloud/Box subscriptions and a small escrow fee on the URF.
*   **Break-Even Point:** Projected at **~700 active lab subscriptions**.

### 11. Compliance & Enterprise Readiness

*   **Data Privacy & Ethics:** The platform is designed to be GDPR & HIPAA ready, with an IRB toolkit and AI transparency banners.
*   **Accessibility:** Forge Studio will include a built-in auditor for WCAG 2.2 / Section 508 compliance.
*   **Data Residency:** The Forge Box appliance offers a region-locked, on-premises solution for labs with strict data sovereignty requirements.

### 12. Risks & Mitigations (Revised)

| Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Browser Timer Throttling** | High | High | Kernel-level detection and warning overlay (KER-005); auto-retry on visibility change API. |
| **LLM Patch Injection** | Medium | High | Zod schema validation + sandboxed execution of patches in a Web Worker with SES hardening; API rate-limiting. |
| **Alpha Users Blocked by Firewalls**| Medium | Medium | Provide a self-contained, downloadable VM image of the Forge Box for initial trials. Automated CSP/SRI checks in CI. |
| **Open-Source Dilution Fork** | Low | Medium | Formal FEP process, Contributor License Agreement (CLA), and strong trademark protection for "ForgeScript" and "Psycho-Forge". |

### 13. Appendix: Initial 10-Sprint Execution Plan

*This plan has been re-baselined to include critical pre-alpha work and to balance sprint loads.*
**(Note: New/revised tickets are marked with ✱)**

**Sprint 0: Foundation (24 pts)**
- `INF-001`: Stand up Monorepo & CI/CD
- `INF-002✱`: Define CSP/SRI & Security Header Contract
- `DSL-001`: Define Initial AST Schema (Zod)
- `DOC-001`: Draft FEP-0000 Governance Model
- `ARC-001`: Spike: Kernel-to-Renderer PoC

**Sprint 1: Runtime Core (28 pts)**
- `KER-001`: High-Precision Clock
- `KER-002`: Hierarchical Scheduler
- `DSL-002`: Implement FZB Serialization
- `DSL-003a✱`: Spike: `ts-morph` vs `babel` for incremental parsing
- `TST-001`: Timing Benchmark Harness v1

**Sprint 2: Compiler & First Test (26 pts)**
- `DSL-003`: Build TS Parser & Compiler
- `KER-005✱`: Cross-OS Timer Drift Detection
- `ARE-003✱`: Lock v0.1 BIDS-compatible Data Schema
- `KER-003`: Implement `FixationCross` & `KeyPress`
- `DOC-002`: Publish Benchmark White-Paper v0.1

**Sprint 3: GUI View Layer (26 pts)**
- `GUI-001`: Set up SvelteKit Project
- `GUI-002`: Visual Timeline (Read-Only)
- `GUI-003`: Code Pane (Monaco)
- `GUI-015✱`: WCAG Audit & Contrast Theme Pass for core UI
- `INF-005a✱`: Telemetry Pipeline Setup (Sentry)

**Sprint 4: Live Mirror-Canvas (27 pts)**
- `GUI-005`: Integrate Automerge State Store
- `GUI-006`: Visual-to-AST Binding
- `GUI-007`: AST-to-Code Binding
- `INF-005b✱`: Instrument Studio with Telemetry Hooks

**Sprint 5: Simulation & Intelligence (29 pts)**
- `SIM-001`: Package `@psyforge/headless` (WASM) & Memory Budget Test
- `GUI-009`: Time-Travel Scrubber UI
- `AI-001`: AI Command Palette Backend
- `GUI-010`: AI Command Palette UI Integration

**Sprint 6: Recruitment & Auth (28 pts)**
- `SRV-001`: Build ForgeRouter Service
- `SRV-002`: Global State DB & Transactional Logic
- `SEC-001✱`: OAuth2 PKCE Flow (Studio ↔ Keycloak)
- `URF-001`: Implement `@psyforge/recruit-bridge` for Prolific

**Sprint 7: Ecosystem Foundations (27 pts)**
- `HUB-001`: Set up Gitea for Forge Hub
- `HUB-002`: Hub API (Read-Only) & UI
- `DAL-001`: Finalize DAL Interfaces
- `DAL-002`: Virtual Device Lab & Keyboard Driver
- `EGS-002`: Begin "100-in-100" Sprint (10 paradigms)

**Sprint 8: Full-Cycle Workflow (27 pts)**
- `MSG-001`: Methods Section Generator
- `ARE-001`: Analysis-Ready Export (Tidy CSV)
- `GUI-012`: Add Export Menu
- `DOC-003`: Write "Literate Design" Guide

**Sprint 9: Polish & Alpha Prep (29 pts)**
- `GUI-013`: Implement RAPD Framework
- `GUI-014`: Build "First-Run Orbit" Demo
- `ARE-002✱`: R Script Code Generation
- `TST-003`: E2E Test: Prolific Integration
- `MKT-001`: Record 3 "Paradigm-in-a-Tweet" Videos

**Sprint 10: Alpha Release (29 pts)**
- `REL-001`: Cut Alpha Release Candidate
- `INF-004`: Deploy Alpha to Production
- `DOC-005`: Finalize Onboarding Docs
- `CSM-001`: Onboard 3 Founding Labs
- `INF-004b✱`: Add License Headers & CLA Bot to Repos ::
